In my experience, deserialization is the time when you generally *don't* know what version to expect, unless you send the version out of band, which seems unnecessary given that we can determine the version by inspecting the encoded macaroon.

OK. So, `transport` and `protocol` are already public attributes on the `Connection`, so the client can come back and override any defaults by pulling the socket out from the transport:

```python
cn = yield from connect()
sock = cn.protocol.get('socket')
sock.setsockopt(...)
```

So you _can_ access the socket directly already to override any defaults. So perhaps the way to go is to simply set the default in `connect` (**without** a parameter to override it) and let clients change the socket after the fact. Are there any restrictions on what you can do to a socket after it's been opened?

If the client has explicitly passed in a socket (`sock is not None`) then we shouldn't change any of its options.

The preference order is a way for us to check for signature versions that we explicitly have signers for. If a service came out with a signer that we didn't understand, then none of our preferred signers would match and we'd use sigv4. The preferred signers order is checked in a specific order to capture how Botocore favors signature versions. We would use v2 for services that only have v2 support, but only if explicitly stated and only as a last resort. The preference order is SDK specific because other SDKs use the same data source but might prefer v4 over s3 (e.g., PHP and Ruby).

> But for the normal integration method, I think it's more complicated than this.  Users have to choose one of the integration methods like sample integrator or continuous integrator, and  either the midpoint or trapezoidal method. 

In practice, there is really just 'trapezoid' and 'midpoint' there too.  If the bandpass or sed has a wave_list, then it is pretty ill-advised to use the ContinuousIntegrator, which will rebin the wavelength range into 250 (or N) equally spaced partitions.  It's almost always better to use the actual wavelengths where the sed and/or bandpass are sampled.  And if there is no wave_list for either of them, then it's not possible to use SampleIntegrator.  So in both cases, the only real question is whether to use trapezoid or midpoint rule for the integration.  

But also, I'm not suggesting removing the existing option to provide an integrator object if the user wants.  I think that should still be an option for advanced users who want to do something a bit unusual.  I'm just suggesting that the default be switched from `integrator=None` to `integrator='trapezoidal'` and the code that checks the input `integrator` change to:
```
if isinstance(integrator, str):
    if integrator == 'trapezoidal':
        rule = np.trapz
    elif integrator == 'midpoint'
        rule = galsim.integ.midpt
    if len(wave_list) > 0:
        integrator = galsim.integ.SampleIntegrator(rule)
    else:
        integrator = galsim.integ.ContinuousIntegrator(rule)
```

Unless users will generally access this multiple times, that's not going to be faster overall.  (Although the slow down is, of course, minuscule...)

I've been looking over this code and trying to determine why this feels like it makes `SLinux` API feel heavy. I am not sure that modifying the loading process to add an entry is the cleanest way to go; otherwise if we add future support for other methods of initialization, it'll grow it further. After some thought, what if we do the following:

Leave the loading process as it was, and add a `set_entry()` method that takes a virtual address.[1] You can then use that and `_find_symbol()` below (or potentially renamed to `_resolve_symbol()`) to alter the entry point after initialization. This way, we also won't introduce ambiguous parameters (`entry`/`entrysymbol`/etc) to the constructor and our elf loading process more closely resembles the actual process in Linux. 

So new initialization will roughly look like:
```python
platform = linux.SLinux(platform, argv, etc...)
if args.entrysymbol and args.entry:
    # raise an exception and notify that they should be mutually exclusive
if args.entrysymbol:
    entry = platform._find_symbol(args.entrysymbol)
    platform.set_entry(entry)
if args.entry:
    platform.set_entry(args.entry)
initial_state = State(constraints, platform)
...
etc
```

What are your thoughts?

[1] We might be able to extend this API to other platforms.

Yes, we should document it. sessions_id is associated with each specific client. It's entirely unrelated to the session rest API. The session_id here is the message protocol `header.session` field, which is used on replies and iopub to identify the original sender of the message. The main *important* use of this session_id is setting zmq.IDENTITY on reconnects to ensure that replies intended for this client still arrive there after reconnects. It's also used to build messages created in the notebook server for this client, but I'm not sure we should commit to or rely on that yet.

The main difficulty I had when debugging all of this was that when celery tasks fail from uncaught exceptions, they don't log anything by default; the tasks just silently die.  So I wrapped this call in a try/catch, and logged the exceptions to diagnose the issues noted below.

The celery-standard way to do this is to subclass `celery.Task`, override the `on_failure` method, and log the exception there.  See edx-platform's [instructor task](https://github.com/edx/edx-platform/blob/master/lms/djangoapps/instructor_task/tasks_base.py) for an example, cf https://stackoverflow.com/a/45333231.

I'd recommend doing that now, so that when you adjust the conditions for updating aggregators, you've got a better chance of catching when things go wrong.

However, the bigger problem is that these tests do not verify the changes to pyflakes.

These tests can run on master, and they all pass, as they are in fact very similar to other functions we have elsewhere in the test suite.

To test your changes, tests need to

1. invoke `Checker` with a `scope` set to a value other than `ModuleScope` .  This can be done with `self.flakes( ... , scope=FunctionScope)`

2. *and* need to pass `Checker` the equivalent of `body[0]` in the issue https://github.com/PyCQA/pyflakes/issues/338 , but that cant be done with `self.flakes(..)` currently.  You might change the harness so that `input` can be either an `ast.Node`, or a string.

3. Inspect the deadScopes , similar to `test_scope_class`, to ensure it has the expected structure , on any version of Python, and any differences between Python versions are recorded into the test suite so they do not flippantly change in the future.

By "tearing" I refer to the phenomenon by which a read of a 64-bit number can result in the lower 32 bits coming from one point in time and the higher 32 bits from another point in time.

As far as I know, the atomic primitives provided by Doug and the JSR-166 team are more targeted toward writes/CAS than reads—indeed, [the OpenJDK implementation of `AtomicInteger#get()`](http://www.grepcode.com/file/repository.grepcode.com/java/root/jdk/openjdk/8u40-b25/java/util/concurrent/atomic/AtomicInteger.java#AtomicInteger.get%28%29) does not lock. (As you pointed out, this doesn't mean that the lock isn't required in Python.)

I have two thoughts on this (which are really two different ways of looking at the same thing:
1. As a reader, when I look at this code, I see the `get_future_exception`, see that it has three possible return values and then am wondering why no result result is being treated as success and wondering if it was mistake because two of the return values happen to be false-y (looking at it through a rust lens, I'd expect there two be three case in handling the return value). Given that the you expect the `False` case never to happen, I'd be fine with that branch being marked `pragma: nocover`.
2. Looking at `get_future_exception`, it looks like a fairly trivial wrapper around `future.exception()`. It looks like it exists to turn a `InvalidStateError` into a `False` return value. As a reader, I expect that there is going to be somewhere where this case is not exception and you want to handle all three case in parallel ways. But when I go look at the only caller, it appears that you don't actually handle the `False` case because it is shouldn't ever happen. That suggests that just using `future.exception()` would be better here, and let the impossible exception bubble up, rather than be silently treated as a success.
